text.title: Automation Audit and Prioritization
text.summary: Time audit process, task categorization, ROI formula, and prioritization matrix for selecting automation targets.

rule.statement: Track all activities for one week before selecting automation targets. Do not automate based on memory — track actual time spent.
rule.rationale: Perceived time cost is unreliable. People underestimate daily tasks (done automatically) and overestimate rare tasks (memorable but infrequent). One week of tracking reveals the actual cost distribution.

list.item: Repetitive: same email, same data entry, same lookup — automate
Sequential: multi-step processes triggered by an event — automate
Scheduled: daily or weekly recurring tasks — automate
Decision-heavy: strategy, creative judgment — do not automate
Relationship: sales calls, personal support — partially automate (prep, follow-up)

section.title: Automation ROI Formula

rule.statement: Only build automations with a projected ROI greater than 5x in the first year. Below this threshold, maintenance overhead typically exceeds the time saved.
rule.rationale: Automations require ongoing maintenance (tool updates, API changes, logic fixes). Low-ROI automations become net-negative over a 12-month period when maintenance is factored in.

section.p: ROI = (time saved per occurrence x occurrences per month) / (build time + monthly maintenance time). Target: >5x annual return.

list.item: High priority: daily task, 30+ minutes → automate first
High priority: error-prone process with costly mistakes (payment, data sync)
Medium priority: weekly task, 1+ hour → automate second
Medium priority: impacts customer experience directly
Low priority: rare task (less than monthly)
Skip: nice-to-have convenience with minimal time cost

text.reference: Weekly time audit template with task categories
